mapping.a_element: growth of the number of distinct tags (global or local vocabulary size) over time
mapping.b_element: average number of distinct sites visited <S_N(t)> by N random walkers up to time t

mapping.a_element: power-law growth of vocabulary size with exponent smaller than one (sub-linear growth)
mapping.b_element: series expansion in ln^{-n}(N) ln^m ln(N) for the territory explored by random walkers, indicating sub-linear or logarithmic corrections to scaling

mapping.a_element: time as the independent variable driving the growth process
mapping.b_element: time t as the independent variable in the random walk process

brief_justification: While both abstracts describe sub-linear growth processes (vocabulary size in tagging systems and territory explored by random walkers), the underlying mechanisms differ: Paper A attributes growth to cognitive and collaborative dynamics with power-law scaling, whereas Paper B derives its results from probabilistic random walk theory with logarithmic corrections. The mathematical structures (power-law vs. logarithmic series) and driving processes (human interaction vs. stochastic particle motion) are distinct.
